name: Paper and Ink Media
tagline: Thin cellulose sheets and fluid pigments that make writing lightweight, foldable, and mass-producible
problem:
Clay is heavy; parchment is expensive; bamboo slips shuffle apart. Paper — fibrillated cellulose matted into sheets — offers a portable, cuttable substrate that accepts ink without immediate bleed-through if sized.

Manufacturing must beat pests: silverfish, mould, and acidic lignin that embrittles sheets. Inks must not fade instantly nor corrode fibres — iron gall ink taught archivists about acidity centuries before pH meters.

Paper made bureaucracy portable enough for empires and later for printed broadsides — a logistics revolution disguised as stationery.
overview:
Traditional paper pulps rags or wood, beats fibres in water, forms sheets on moulds, presses, and dries. Sizing with gelatin or starch reduces feathering. East Asian papers used mulberry and rice straw; Islamic mills refined rag paper; European industrialisation added Hollander beaters and Fourdrinier machines.

Inks span carbon soot binders, iron gall, and later aniline dyes. Fountain pens demanded controlled surface tension; ballpoints needed quick-drying oils.

Archival practice deacidifies; stores in climate control; digitises as insurance — paper is mortal.

For recovery, rag supply chains and clear water matter as much as presses.

principles.name: Hydrogen bonding network
principles.explanation: Cellulose fibres mat through hydrogen bonds when drained — strength scales with beating energy and fibre length.

principles.name: Sizing chemistry
principles.explanation: Fill pores partially so ink sits atop; oversizing repels ink — balance is empirical.

principles.name: Lignin yellowing
principles.explanation: Mechanical wood pulp retains lignin that oxidises — newsprint is ephemeral by chemistry.

principles.name: Capillary wicking
principles.explanation: Porosity and fibre orientation set anisotropic spread — grain direction matters for folding and printing.

components.name: Pulp slurry
components.madeFrom: Rag or wood chips after cook

rawMaterials.name: Pigments
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Disperse to submicron to avoid clogging

buildSteps.title: Fibre preparation
buildSteps.description: Sort rags; remove buttons; cook with lime if needed; beat to fibrillation target.

buildSteps.title: Sheet formation
buildSteps.description: Vat dip or machine lay; control basis weight with consistency loop.
buildSteps.warningNote: Uneven couching tears on printing press

buildSteps.title: Press and dry
buildSteps.description: Gradual drying prevents cockling; restraint drying for flatness.

buildSteps.title: Size and polish
buildSteps.description: Brush gelatin size; calendar if high gloss needed.
buildSteps.warningNote: Acidic alum size ages poorly — track pH

buildSteps.title: Test print
buildSteps.description: Proof with intended ink and press pressure; adjust absorbency.
buildSteps.warningNote: Coated paper needs different inks than offset stock

history.event: Cai Lun associated with papermaking improvement in Chinese tradition.
Paper mill established in Xàtiva region — spreads in Islamic world and Europe.

inventors.name: Cai Lun
inventors.contribution: Traditional attribution for Han-era papermaking improvements

impact: Paper lowered the mass-per-word of bureaucracy and scholarship — prerequisite for printing’s economics and for modern notebooks that think alongside engineers.

videos.title: Hand papermaking
videos.description: Craft demonstrations of mould and deckle technique.
